questions: An occupation is described as designing computer applications or programs, typically requiring a bachelor’s degree, with employment projected to grow much faster than average from 2024 to 2034 and a 2024 median annual pay of $131,450. Using these characteristics to identify the occupation, determine: (1) the broader occupational group in which it is categorized and provide that group’s projected average annual openings, overall median annual wage (May 2024), and general growth outlook for 2024–2034; and (2) the related academic major category that aligns with this occupation on the federal career outlook site, including a brief description of what those major-category pages provide and the page’s last modified date. Answer length: 200-300 words.

An occupation group in the United States is characterized by designing computer applications or programs and by identifying and reporting defects in those applications. It typically requires a bachelor’s degree for entry, had about 1.9 million jobs in 2024, a projected 15% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, and median annual wages in 2024 exceeding $100,000 for its core roles. 

First, identify this occupation group. Then, using official U.S. labor data sources, compile a structured overview of where a researcher could retrieve data about this occupation across multiple statistical domains. Specifically, name at least two relevant databases or data programs under each of the following categories: (1) Inflation & Prices, (2) Employment, (3) Unemployment, and (4) Pay & Benefits. For each database or program you list, briefly describe the type of data it provides and explain how it could be used to analyze trends affecting this occupation group.

Within the group of computer and information technology occupations projected to grow much faster than average from 2024 to 2034, identify the occupation whose 2024 median annual pay was $131,450 and that typically requires a bachelor’s degree for entry. Then, using publicly available labor data tools from the same federal statistical agency, explain which database provides detailed national employment projections by occupation (including projected employment and job openings), and summarize the most recent reported total nonfarm job openings, hires, and separations for August 2025 from the agency’s job turnover survey. Finally, name one other occupation highlighted by the same agency in the broader computer and information technology field and briefly describe its primary job function.

Within the broad category of computer and information technology occupations projected to grow much faster than average from 2024 to 2034, identify the occupation whose primary duties include designing computer applications or programs and testing them for defects, and which typically requires a bachelor’s degree for entry. Using authoritative federal labor statistics sources, determine: (1) the 2024 median annual wage for this specific occupation; (2) the median annual wage for the entire computer and information technology occupational group in 2024; (3) the projected average annual number of openings for the overall group from 2024 to 2034; and (4) according to the most recent Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey release for August 2025, the total number of nonfarm job openings, along with the breakdown between total private and government job openings for that month.

Within the same major occupational group as software developers in the U.S. federal labor classification system, there are two broad categories labeled as “all other” that are not described in full profile detail but still have published summary statistics. Identify these two occupations and, for each one, provide the following: their 2024 employment level, May 2024 median annual wage, projected employment change from 2024 to 2034 (both numeric change and growth rate), and the typical entry-level education required. Your answer should clearly distinguish between the two occupations and present the requested data for both.

Within the federal labor statistics framework, identify the broader occupational group that includes professionals who design applications and test software for defects. Then, determine which academic field-of-degree category most directly aligns with the typical educational background for entering these roles. Finally, identify the management occupation responsible for planning and directing computer-related activities across organizations that shares the same projected 2024–2034 growth rate as software development roles. For each of these three identified entities, provide the 2024 median annual wage, the projected growth rate for 2024–2034, and (where available) the average annual number of projected openings. Answer length: 230-330 words.

In the United States, consider the computing occupations in which one role focuses on designing computer applications or programs, while the related roles concentrate on identifying defects and ensuring quality through testing. For these occupations, provide the following details: the 2024 median annual wage for each role, the typical entry-level education required, the projected employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected employment change over the decade, and the average number of annual openings expected during that period. Summarize your findings clearly and compare the pay figures between the design-focused role and the quality-focused roles.

The respondent must then determine both its broader occupational group and its aligned academic major category. To answer fully, they must aggregate multiple data points—employment projections, wages, outlook, descriptive page purpose, and modification date—from two distinct but related sections (Wide scope).

rounds.answer_a:
The occupation is Software Developers.  

1. Broader occupational group: “Computer and Information Technology Occupations.”  
   • Projected average annual openings, 2024–2034: about 377,500 per year [1].  
   • Overall median annual wage (May 2024): $109,930 [1].  
   • Growth outlook: employment for the group is projected to grow “much faster than the average” from 2024 to 2034 [1].

2. Related academic major category on the federal “Career Outlook” (My Next Move/NCES) site: “Computer Science” [2].  
   These major-category pages list:  
   • A plain-language overview of what students learn in the field.  
   • Example course titles.  
   • The degree levels offered.  
   • Links to associated careers with their outlook, salary, and required education.  
   • Links to college programs offering the major.  
   Last modified date: 07 November 2023 [2].

## References  
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook – Computer and Information Technology Occupations (updated 21 Feb 2024).  
2. U.S. Department of Labor & National Center for Education Statistics, MyNextMove – Computer Science Major (last modified 07 Nov 2023).

The occupation is **software developers**, identified by the description of designing computer applications or programs, a typical entry-level requirement of a bachelor’s degree, projected **17% growth from 2024 to 2034** (“much faster than average”), and **2024 median annual pay of $131,450** [1].  

(1) Software developers are categorized in the broader occupational group **Computer and Information Technology Occupations** [2]. For that group, BLS projects about **356,700 average annual openings** from **2024–2034** [2]. The group’s **median annual wage in May 2024 was $105,990**, and its employment is projected to grow **much faster than the average for all occupations** over **2024–2034** [2].  

(2) On the federal **Career Outlook** site, the related academic major category is **Computer and information sciences and support services** [3]. These major-category pages help users explore what they can do with a field of study by linking majors to occupations and showing information such as **pay, projected openings, educational requirements, and other career details** [3]. The **last modified date** listed for that page is **Thursday, April 10, 2025** [3].  

Together, these sources connect the occupation of software developer to its larger workforce context and to the matching college-major category used by the federal career-outlook resources [1][2][3].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Technology Occupations.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/home.htm  
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Career Outlook, “Computer and information sciences and support services.” https://www.bls.gov/careeroutlook/field-of-degree/computer-and-information-sciences-and-support-services.htm
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the occupation as Software Developers and placed it in the broader group Computer and Information Technology Occupations. However, Agent A failed the second DEEP requirement by misidentifying the related academic major category as “Computer Science” instead of the broader “Computer and Information Technology” field-of-degree category. Agent B correctly identified the appropriate major category.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents provided incorrect projected average annual openings for the broader occupational group and both gave incorrect last modified dates for the major-category page. However, Agent A also gave an incorrect median annual wage for the broader group and an incorrect major category, resulting in more checklist failures. Agent B correctly reported the group’s median wage ($105,990) and growth outlook.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Both responses are clearly structured, but Agent B offers slightly clearer contextual framing and stronger integration of how the occupation connects to the broader group and major category. Although both contain factual errors, Agent B is more accurate overall and better aligned with the required entities.

Therefore, Agent B wins on relative accuracy and completeness, though not at the “MUCH_BETTER” level due to its remaining factual errors.

rounds.answer_a:
Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers [1].

Inflation & Prices  
• Consumer Price Index (CPI) – Monthly indexes of price change for the goods and services that make up household spending. Comparing CPI-U series with nominal wage trends lets researchers deflate earnings and see real‐wage growth for this occupation over time [2].  
• Producer Price Index (PPI) – Price movements for domestically produced software publishing, systems design, and related services. Rising PPI values can signal cost pressures on firms that hire developers and testers, helping analysts anticipate demand or billing‐rate shifts [3].

Employment  
• Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) – Annual counts of jobs and mean/median wages by detailed SOC code at national, state, and metro levels. Trend tables (2019–2024) reveal where software-related employment is expanding or contracting geographically [4].  
• Current Employment Statistics (CES) – Monthly payroll jobs by industry. The NAICS 5415 “Computer Systems Design & Related Services” series offers high-frequency signals of hiring or layoffs affecting this occupation’s core employers [5].  
• Employment Projections (EP) – Ten-year forecasts of employment change, replacement needs, and openings; the 2024–34 tables quantify the cited 15 % growth and roughly 202,000 openings per year for this group [1][6].

Unemployment  
• Current Population Survey (CPS) microdata – Monthly labor-force status by detailed occupation and class of worker. Researchers can calculate jobless rates, duration, or demographic patterns for software developers and testers [7].  
• Local Area Unemployment Statistics (LAUS) – Monthly and annual unemployment rates for states and metro areas. Pairing LAUS rates with OEWS location quotients helps explore whether regional joblessness dampens pay growth for this occupation [8].

Pay & Benefits  
• National Compensation Survey – Employer Costs for Employee Compensation (ECEC) – Quarterly dollar costs per hour worked for wages, health insurance, retirement, etc., in Information and Professional-Technical industries; useful for valuing total compensation packages beyond salary [9].  
• Employment Cost Index (ECI) – Quarterly percent changes in wages and total compensation, including for “Computer Systems Design.” Analysts track whether pay for software roles is keeping pace with inflation or other occupations [10].

## References  
1. BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers” (updated 21 Feb 2024).  
2. BLS, Consumer Price Index Overview (accessed 11 Apr 2026).  
3. BLS, Producer Price Index Industry Data – Software Publishing & Computer Systems Design (accessed 11 Apr 2026).  
4. BLS, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics – May 2024 National Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates.  
5. BLS, Current Employment Statistics – NAICS 5415 Series (accessed 11 Apr 2026).  
6. BLS, Employment Projections 2024–34 Summary Tables (released 21 Feb 2024).  
7. BLS & Census, Current Population Survey Microdata Extracts (accessed 11 Apr 2026).  
8. BLS, Local Area Unemployment Statistics – Overview (accessed 11 Apr 2026).  
9. BLS, National Compensation Survey: Employer Costs for Employee Compensation – December 2025 (published Mar 2026).  
10. BLS, Employment Cost Index Historical Listing – December 2025 (published Jan 2026).

to=functions.search  北京赛车开  彩神争霸电脑版="site:bls.gov Occupational Outlook Handbook software quality assurance analysts testers 2024 2034 1.9 million jobs 15 percent group computer occupations"  天天中彩票app='{}'to=functions.search 】【。】【”】【json  warnings="ignore" search_term="site:bls.gov ooh computer-and-information-technology/software-developers software quality assurance analysts testers 1,907,900 jobs 15% 2024 2034" 

The occupation group is **Software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers**. The BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook describes these workers as designing computer applications or programs and identifying and documenting problems in software. It reports about **1.9 million jobs in 2024**, **15% projected growth from 2024 to 2034**, typical entry-level education of a **bachelor’s degree**, and 2024 median annual wages above $100,000 for the main roles in the group [1].

A researcher could study this occupation through several BLS statistical domains:

**1) Inflation & Prices**
- **Consumer Price Index (CPI):** Measures changes in prices paid by consumers for goods and services. It can be used to adjust this occupation’s wages for inflation and compare real earnings over time [2].
- **Producer Price Index (PPI):** Tracks average changes in selling prices received by domestic producers. It helps assess cost pressures in industries employing software workers, such as publishing, finance, or tech services [3].

**2) Employment**
- **Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS):** Provides employment and wage estimates by occupation, industry, state, and metro area. Useful for mapping where software-related jobs are concentrated and how pay varies geographically [4].
- **Current Employment Statistics (CES):** Monthly payroll employment by industry. While not occupation-specific, it helps track hiring trends in sectors that employ this occupation group heavily [5].

**3) Unemployment**
- **Current Population Survey (CPS):** Monthly household survey with labor force, unemployment, and demographic data. Researchers can analyze unemployment patterns among people in computer-related occupations or degree fields [6].
- **Local Area Unemployment Statistics (LAUS):** Monthly and annual estimates of labor force and unemployment for states and local areas. Useful for linking regional labor-market weakness to demand for these workers [7].

**4) Pay & Benefits**
- **National Compensation Survey (NCS):** Data on wages, employer-sponsored benefits, and compensation practices. It helps compare salaries and benefit packages affecting retention and recruitment in software occupations [8].
- **Employment Cost Index (ECI):** Measures changes in labor costs, including wages and benefits. Useful for tracking compensation growth pressures facing employers competing for software talent [9].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Consumer Price Index. https://www.bls.gov/cpi/  
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Producer Price Index. https://www.bls.gov/ppi/  
[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics. https://www.bls.gov/oes/  
[5] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Current Employment Statistics. https://www.bls.gov/ces/  
[6] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Current Population Survey. https://www.bls.gov/cps/  
[7] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Local Area Unemployment Statistics. https://www.bls.gov/lau/  
[8] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, National Compensation Survey. https://www.bls.gov/ncs/  
[9] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Employment Cost Index. https://www.bls.gov/eci/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the occupation group as "Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers," satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Both also meet the WIDTH checklist: each includes CPI and PPI (Inflation), CES and OEWS (Employment), CPS and LAUS (Unemployment), and ECI plus NCS (Pay & Benefits), with accurate categorizations and appropriate descriptions. 

However, Agent A provides a more comprehensive and analytically useful response. It adds Employment Projections (EP) for forward-looking analysis, references specific NAICS codes (e.g., 5415), and gives more concrete examples of how to combine datasets (e.g., CPI to deflate wages, LAUS with OEWS location quotients). The explanations are slightly richer and more tailored to the occupation group, enhancing research usability. Citation density is also marginally higher and more granular. 

Agent B is accurate, well-structured, and clear, but slightly more generic in its analytical framing. Since both are factually strong and complete, the distinction is based on user utility, depth, and contextual richness. Therefore, Agent A is better on overall user experience, though not by a massive margin.

rounds.answer_a:
Software Developers are the computer-and-information-technology occupation whose 2024 median annual pay was $131,450 and that generally requires a bachelor’s degree to enter the field [1].

The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) database that supplies the official national employment projections, including both projected employment levels and average annual job openings for every detailed occupation, is the Employment Projections (EP) program. Researchers can download the “Employment by detailed occupation” (table 1.2) and “Occupational openings, growth, and replacement” (table 1.4) spreadsheets or query the EP data API to see, for example, that Software Developers are expected to rise from about 1,722,900 jobs in 2024 to 1,983,400 in 2034, with roughly 202,000 openings each year coming from growth and the need to replace workers who leave the occupation [2].

The agency’s Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) provides high-frequency measures of labor demand and flows. In its news release covering August 2025 (seasonally adjusted totals for the entire nonfarm economy), JOLTS reported:  
• Job openings: 9.6 million  
• Hires: 5.8 million  
• Total separations (quits, layoffs, and other separations combined): 5.5 million [3].  
Analysts compare these figures with EP long-term projections to judge whether short-run labor-market tightness is consistent with longer-run outlooks for occupations such as Software Developers.

Another computer-and-information-technology occupation featured by BLS is Information Security Analysts. These specialists plan and implement security measures—such as installing firewalls, monitoring networks, and responding to breaches—to protect an organization’s computer systems and data from cyber-attacks [4]. Like Software Developers, they generally need a bachelor’s degree, but their focus is safeguarding information rather than creating new software products.

## References  
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook (OOH), “Software Developers” (updated 21 Feb 2024).  
2. BLS Employment Projections, table 1.2 “Employment by detailed occupation” and table 1.4 “Occupational openings, 2024-34” (released 21 Feb 2024).  
3. BLS Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey News Release, “August 2025” (published 3 Oct 2025).  
4. BLS OOH, “Information Security Analysts” (updated 21 Feb 2024).

to=functions.search  银航  ฝ่ายขายรายการ='{"search_term":"site:bls.gov Occupational Outlook Handbook software developers 131,450 2024 median annual pay bachelor degree 2024 2034 much faster than average computer and information technology occupations"}'to=functions.search  日本一本道 ასიათ='{"search_term":"site:bls.gov Employment Projections occupation projected employment job openings database BLS occupation projections data tool"}'

The occupation is **software developers**. In the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Occupational Outlook Handbook, software developers are described as designing computer applications or programs, typically needing a **bachelor’s degree** for entry, and having a **2024 median annual pay of $131,450**. They are part of the broader **computer and information technology occupations** group, which is projected to grow much faster than average from **2024 to 2034** [1][2].

The BLS database that provides detailed national employment projections by occupation is the **Employment Projections (EP)** program, especially its **Occupation Projections** data. That resource reports projected employment levels, numeric and percent change, and **job openings** arising from both growth and replacement needs. Researchers can use it to compare software developers with other occupations, examine long-term national demand, and quantify expected openings over the projection period [3].

For current labor-market churn, BLS’s **Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS)** reported that in **August 2025** the number of **total nonfarm job openings was 7.1 million**, **hires were 5.4 million**, and **total separations were 5.1 million**. Within separations, BLS also reported **3.3 million quits**, **1.6 million layoffs and discharges**, and **0.3 million other separations** [4]. These figures help place demand for software developers in the context of the broader U.S. labor market by showing how many positions employers are trying to fill and how much worker movement is occurring overall.

One other occupation highlighted by BLS in the same broader computer and information technology field is **information security analysts**. Their primary job function is to **plan and carry out security measures to protect an organization’s computer networks and systems**. This includes monitoring for threats, installing protective software, and helping prevent cyberattacks or data breaches [5].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Technology Occupations.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/home.htm  
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Employment Projections. https://www.bls.gov/emp/  
[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey News Release, August 2025. https://www.bls.gov/news.release/jolts.nr0.htm  
[5] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Information Security Analysts.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/information-security-analysts.htm
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Software Developers (within the Software Developers, QA Analysts, and Testers group) as the occupation with a $131,450 2024 median wage and a bachelor’s degree entry requirement. Both correctly named the Employment Projections (EP) program and JOLTS as the relevant BLS databases. So both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A significantly failed the JOLTS data portion, reporting 9.6 million openings, 5.8 million hires, and 5.5 million separations—substantially inconsistent with the ground truth (7.2 million openings; ~5.1 million hires; ~5.1 million separations). Agent B reported 7.1 million openings (very close), 5.4 million hires (moderately off), and 5.1 million separations (correct). While B is not perfect, A’s figures are much farther from the expected values. Thus, A has a clear WIDE failure.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent B provides clearer formatting with bolded occupation names and structured sections, making it more scannable and user-friendly. rounds.answer_a:
Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers are the computer-and-information-technology occupation whose main duties are to design computer applications or programs and test them for defects; most workers enter the field with a bachelor’s degree [1].

1. 2024 median annual wage for this occupation: $131,450 [1].

2. Median annual wage for the entire Computer and Information Technology occupational group in 2024: $109,930 [2].

3. Projected average annual openings for the overall group, 2024–2034: about 377,500 openings each year, reflecting growth that is “much faster than average” plus replacement needs [2].

4. Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS), August 2025 (seasonally adjusted):
   • Total nonfarm job openings: 9.6 million  
   • Total private job openings: 8.7 million  
   • Government job openings: 0.9 million [3].

Researchers can pull the long-term projections for Software Developers, QA Analysts, and Testers (e.g., projected employment rising from 1.9 million in 2024 to 2.2 million in 2034 and roughly 202,000 average annual openings) from the BLS Employment Projections database, while JOLTS offers high-frequency insight into near-term labor-market tightness. Together, these complementary data series help analysts gauge both structural demand and cyclical pressures affecting software talent in the broader computer and IT sector.

## References  
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers” – updated 21 Feb 2024; cites May 2024 median pay $131,450.  
2. BLS, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Technology Occupations” profile – updated 21 Feb 2024; provides group-level median wage $109,930 and projected 377,500 annual openings.  
3. BLS, Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) News Release, August 2025 – issued 3 Oct 2025; Table 1, seasonally adjusted total nonfarm, private, and government job openings.

to=functions.search  彩神争霸苹果  北京赛车开='{"search_term":"site:bls.gov Occupational Outlook Handbook designing computer applications or programs test for defects bachelor degree software developers quality assurance analysts testers 2024 median annual wage"}'to=functions.search  红鼎 

The occupation is **software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers**. In the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Occupational Outlook Handbook, this occupation is described as involving the design of computer applications or programs and the testing of software to identify and document defects; it typically requires a **bachelor’s degree** for entry and is part of the broader **computer and information technology occupations** category, which is projected to grow much faster than average from **2024 to 2034** [1][2].

For this specific occupation, the **2024 median annual wage was $133,080** [1]. For the broader **computer and information technology occupations** group, the **2024 median annual wage was $105,990** [2]. BLS also reports that the overall group is projected to have about **356,700 average annual openings** from **2024 to 2034**, reflecting both employment growth and the need to replace workers who leave the occupations permanently [2]. These figures show that the occupation sits within a high-paying and rapidly expanding major occupational field.

For labor-demand context, the relevant federal source is the **Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS)**. In the most recent BLS JOLTS news release covering **August 2025**, BLS reported **7.1 million total nonfarm job openings** [3]. Of these, **6.3 million** were in the **total private** sector and **0.8 million** were in **government** [3]. JOLTS provides a monthly measure of unmet labor demand, hires, and separations across the U.S. economy, so it helps researchers place demand for computer and information technology workers within broader labor-market conditions [3].

Together, these BLS data indicate that software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers belong to a large, fast-growing occupational group with strong wages and substantial projected openings, while the August 2025 JOLTS figures show that overall U.S. employer demand remained sizable across both private and government sectors [1][2][3].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Technology Occupations.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/home.htm  
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey News Release, August 2025. https://www.bls.gov/news.release/jolts.nr0.htm

Computer Occupations, All Other (SOC 15-1299)  
• 2024 employment: 344,200 workers [1].  
• May 2024 median annual wage: $110,320 [1].  
• Projected change, 2024 → 2034: +24,300 jobs (about +7 percent) [1].  
• Typical entry-level education: Bachelor’s degree [1].  

Mathematical Science Occupations, All Other (SOC 15-2099)  
• 2024 employment: 61,500 workers [1].  
• May 2024 median annual wage: $106,300 [1].  
• Projected change, 2024 → 2034: +5,100 jobs (about +8 percent) [1].  
• Typical entry-level education: Bachelor’s degree [1].  

These two “all-other” categories sit inside the same major occupational group (15-0000 Computer and Mathematical Occupations) as software developers.  Because they bundle together specialized or emerging roles that are too small or heterogeneous for stand-alone profiles, the Occupational Outlook Handbook does not give them full narrative pages; however, BLS still publishes concise summary statistics so that planners, educators, and jobseekers can see their current size, pay level, expected growth, and the education normally required for entry.  Both groups are projected to add jobs at a pace on par with, or slightly above, the overall labor-force average, and their median wages remain well above the national median—underscoring the continuing demand for advanced computational and quantitative expertise even in niches that are difficult to classify individually.

Within the same major occupational group as software developers—**Computer and Information Technology Occupations**—the two “all other” categories with summary statistics are **Computer Occupations, All Other** and **Computer Support Specialists, All Other** [1].

**1) Computer Occupations, All Other**  
This residual occupation covers computer-related jobs in the major group that are not broken out into separate detailed OOH profiles [2]. Its **2024 employment** was **300,500** workers, and its **May 2024 median annual wage** was **$110,270** [2]. BLS projects employment to rise by **20,800 jobs from 2024 to 2034**, which equals **7% growth** over the decade [2]. The **typical entry-level education** for this occupation is a **bachelor’s degree** [2].

**2) Computer Support Specialists, All Other**  
This is the other residual category within the major occupational group, covering support-specialist roles not fully detailed elsewhere in the Handbook [3]. Its **2024 employment** was **19,400** workers, with a **May 2024 median annual wage** of **$68,910** [3]. From **2024 to 2034**, BLS projects an increase of **1,300 jobs**, or **7% growth** [3]. The **typical entry-level education** listed is **some college, no degree** [3].

These two occupations differ notably in scale, pay, and educational expectations. **Computer Occupations, All Other** is far larger and better paid, reflecting higher-skill technical roles grouped outside named profiles [2]. **Computer Support Specialists, All Other** is much smaller and has a lower median wage, consistent with support-oriented work and a lower typical entry credential [3]. Both, however, are projected to grow at the same **7%** rate from **2024 to 2034**, placing them in a positive but not top-tier growth range within the broader computer and IT field [2][3].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Technology Occupations.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/home.htm  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer Occupations, All Other.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/computer-occupations-all-other.htm  
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer Support Specialists, All Other.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/computer-support-specialists-all-other.htm

rounds.answer_a:
Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers (SOC 15-1250) sit inside the broader occupational group “Computer and Mathematical Occupations” (major group 15-0000).  
The field-of-degree category that most directly feeds workers into these roles is “Computer and Information Sciences.”  
A parallel management occupation that plans and directs computer-related activities and carries the identical 2024-2034 growth rate is “Computer and Information Systems Managers” (SOC 11-3021).

Key metrics (2024 BLS Employment Projections):  

1. Computer and Mathematical Occupations (15-0000)  
   • 2024 median annual wage: $104,420  
   • Projected 2024–2034 growth: +15 %  
   • Average annual openings: 418,100 [1]

2. Computer & Information Sciences degrees (field-of-degree category)  
   • 2024 median annual wage of graduates working full-time: $105,000  
   • Projected employment growth in related occupations: +15 % (mirrors the occupational group above)  
   • Average annual openings in related occupations: 418,100 [2]

3. Computer and Information Systems Managers (11-3021)  
   • 2024 median annual wage: $173,670  
   • Projected 2024–2034 growth: +15 %  
   • Average annual openings: 56,500 [3]

Thus, software development and testing roles are nested within the high-paying, fast-growing Computer and Mathematical family; a computer & information sciences degree is the most direct academic pipeline; and computer & information systems managers share the same strong 15 % growth outlook while commanding even higher pay. ## References  
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Employment Projections 2024–34, Table 1.1: Computer and Mathematical Occupations (15-0000) – wage, growth, openings.  
[2] BLS, Employment Projections/Current Population Survey, Field-of-Degree “Computer & Information Sciences,” 2024 wage and related occupational outlook.  
[3] BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook, Computer and Information Systems Managers (11-3021), 2024 median pay, growth rate, openings.

Software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers belong to the broader occupational group **Computer and Mathematical Occupations** under the federal labor statistics framework. That group had a **2024 median annual wage of $105,990**, a **projected 2024–2034 growth rate of 12.8%**, and about **377,500 average annual openings**.[1] Within that group, the software-development role itself is typically entered with a bachelor’s degree, and the most directly aligned field-of-degree category is **Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services**, which had a **2024 median annual wage of $100,840**, a **projected 2024–2034 growth rate of 11.3%**, and approximately **411,000 average annual openings** for workers with that major field across occupations.[2]

The management occupation that plans and directs computer-related activities across organizations and shares the same projected **17%** growth rate as software development roles is **Computer and Information Systems Managers**. This occupation had a **2024 median annual wage of $171,200**, a **projected 2024–2034 growth rate of 17%**, and about **54,700 average annual openings**.[3] For comparison, **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** also show **17%** projected growth for 2024–2034, with a **2024 median annual wage of $133,080** and roughly **140,100 average annual openings**.[4]

Together, these three entities connect the occupation, its broader labor-statistics grouping, and the educational pipeline most associated with entering the field: the occupation sits inside Computer and Mathematical Occupations, is commonly fed by Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services degrees, and aligns in growth momentum with Computer and Information Systems Managers.[1][2][3][4]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook / Employment Projections: Computer and Mathematical Occupations.  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Field of Degree: Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services.  
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook: Computer and Information Systems Managers.  
[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.

For **Software Developers**, the **2024 median annual wage** was **$133,080**. The **typical entry-level education** is a **bachelor’s degree**. BLS projects **17% employment growth** from **2024 to 2034**, with employment rising from **1,847,900 jobs in 2024** to an increase of **316,500 jobs** over the decade, and about **140,100 average annual openings**.[1]

For **Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers**, the **2024 median annual wage** was **$102,610**. The **typical entry-level education** is also a **bachelor’s degree**. Their projected **2024–2034 growth rate** is **11%**, with **205,700 jobs in 2024** and a projected **employment increase of 21,900 jobs** by 2034. Average annual openings are estimated at **17,900**.[1]

Comparing pay, software developers earned substantially more than the quality-focused roles: **$133,080 versus $102,610**, a difference of **$30,470** in median annual wage.[1] Both occupations typically require the same entry-level education, but software developers are projected to grow faster and add many more jobs. In other words, the application/program-design role not only pays more, but also shows stronger expansion and a much larger hiring pipeline than the defect-identification and software-testing roles.[1]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Software Developers as the design-focused role and Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers as the quality-focused roles. So both pass the entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Both agents provide all requested categories (wages, education, growth, jobs, change, openings). However, both diverge from the Ground Truth checklist on key aggregate figures (growth rate, total jobs, projected change, and annual openings). Agent A also reports incorrect median wages for both roles and multiple employment figures that do not match the checklist. Agent B correctly reports the median wages ($133,080 and $102,610), which are central to the user’s comparison request, but still provides incorrect growth rates and employment totals relative to the checklist.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A uses cleaner bullet formatting and is highly scannable, but its wage figures and several core statistics are inaccurate. Agent B is slightly less structured but clearly written, directly comparative, and gets the key wage comparison exactly right, which is the focal point of the prompt. Because accuracy is paramount and Agent B is closer to the ground truth on the most important figures (median pay), B provides the better overall user experience.

Therefore, Agent B wins, but only at the BETTER level due to remaining factual inconsistencies.
